abstract:
A three-dimensional network solid, (NBu4)2FeIII2(dhbq)3, composed of iron(III) centers and paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers, is found to exhibit an electrical conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm at 298 K. This value represents one of the highest conductivities reported for a metal-organic framework (MOF) to date. Detailed characterization using spectroscopic techniques, variable-temperature conductivity, magnetic susceptibility, and slow-scan cyclic voltammetry reveals that the electronic transport is driven by organic mixed-valency within the ligand scaffold. Notably, UV-Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra provide the first evidence of Robin-Day Class II/III mixed valency in a MOF. 

Stoichiometric control over the ligand redox states was achieved through chemical reduction to produce the related framework Na0.9(NBu4)1.8FeIII2(dhbq)3. A comparative study between the parent and reduced materials shows that both electronic conductivity and magnetic ordering temperatures are governed by the specific ratio of the ligand redox states. The parent material exhibits ferrimagnetic ordering at low temperatures, with magnetic interactions persisting well above the transition point. These findings establish transition metal-semiquinoid MOFs as a versatile platform for achieving and tuning long-range electronic communication and high-temperature magnetic coupling in porous solids.
